coredata.dc:title: A Type-II CQDs regulating FeOOH decorated BiVO4 photoelectrode for highly efficient photoelectrochemical degradation of tetracycline
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Shuai
Li, Xiaohui
Fan, Qinge
Wang, Penglong
Pu, Yanfeng
Jia, Yushuai
Wang, Li
Chang, Zhixian
Chong, Ruifeng
coredata.dc:description: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) photoelectrode is of great potentials to be used for photoelectrochemical (PEC) removal of antibiotic pollutant. However, it still suffers from lower efficiency. Herein, a type-II FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 photoelectrode was fabricated via the in-situ growth of FeOOH nanoparticles over BiVO4 using Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as mediator. Benefiting from the type-II heterojunction between BiVO4 and FeOOH, as well as the excellent charger transfer assisted by CQDs, FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibited excellent PEC degradation performance towards a typical antibiotic- tetracycline (TC). In a typical PEC process, FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 photoelectrode (2 cm × 2 cm) yielded a high removal ratio of ∼ 99 % toward TC within 120 min at an applied potential of 0.6 V (vs. Hg/Hg2Cl2). Moreover, the PEC configuration was verified to be used for TC degradation in actual waters, such as tap water and lake water, with > 90 % of removal ratio. The radical trapping experiments revealed holes and superoxide radical dominated in the TC degradation process. This work provides an insight on the type-II heterojunction photoelectrode to enhance the PEC degradation of TC, which would help for the treatment of antibiotic wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical
Tetracycline
Bismuth vanadate
Carbon quantum dots
Type-II heterojunction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic preparation process of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4; SEM images of (b) BiVO4, (c) FeOOH/BiVO4 and (d) FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4; (e) Cross-sectional SEM, (f) TEM and (g) HRTEM images of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4.

(a) XRD patterns, (b) UV–Vis DRS and (c) corresponding Tauc plots of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4, FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4; The high-resolution XPS spectra for (d) Bi 4f, (e) Fe 2p and (f) O 1 s in various photoelectrodes.

(a) LSV curves, (b) the half capacitive current difference (Δj/2) vs. the scanning rates, (c) OCP profiles, (d) EIS plots and (e) Mott-Schottky plots of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4 and FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4; (f) The band alignment of the type-II heterojunction between FeOOH and BiVO4. The inset in (d) shows the equivalent circuit for EIS data fitting.

(a) The degradation efficiencies of TC and (b) corresponding kinetic fitting of various photoelectrodes; (c) Recycling experiments and (d) degradation performance in real waters over FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4.

(a) Degradation efficiency and (b) corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting of TC with PEC, EC and PC; (c) trapping experiments and (d) the corresponding histogram of removal efficiency at 120 min.
Proposed degradation pathway of tetracycline in PEC system.
The proposed PEC degradation mechanism of TC over FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4.

head.ce:title: A Type-II CQDs regulating FeOOH decorated BiVO4 photoelectrode for highly efficient photoelectrochemical degradation of tetracycline

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shool of Energy Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) photoelectrode is of great potentials to be used for photoelectrochemical (PEC) removal of antibiotic pollutant. However, it still suffers from lower efficiency. Herein, a type-II FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 photoelectrode was fabricated via the in-situ growth of FeOOH nanoparticles over BiVO4 using Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as mediator. Benefiting from the type-II heterojunction between BiVO4 and FeOOH, as well as the excellent charger transfer assisted by CQDs, FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibited excellent PEC degradation performance towards a typical antibiotic- tetracycline (TC). In a typical PEC process, FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 photoelectrode (2 cm × 2 cm) yielded a high removal ratio of ∼ 99 % toward TC within 120 min at an applied potential of 0.6 V (vs. Hg/Hg2Cl2). Moreover, the PEC configuration was verified to be used for TC degradation in actual waters, such as tap water and lake water, with > 90 % of removal ratio. The radical trapping experiments revealed holes and superoxide radical dominated in the TC degradation process. This work provides an insight on the type-II heterojunction photoelectrode to enhance the PEC degradation of TC, which would help for the treatment of antibiotic wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a great achievement in the history of medicine, antibiotics have been extensively used for preventing human bacterial infections and animal infectious [1]. The inappropriate use, as well as the discharge from industrial effluents, antibiotics have been widely detected in surface water, groundwater and effluent [2]. The antibiotics in water environment could cause the dissemination of antibiotic resistant bacteria, and subsequently cause multiple water-related diseases, posing a severe risk to ecosystem and public health [3,4]. However, owing to the inherent high stability, most antibiotics are difficult to be biodegraded or eliminated via the conventional industrial process, such as biological method, Fenton process, peroxymonosulfate oxidation, electrochemical method and ozone oxidation [5,6].

Recently, photocatalysis was found to be an efficient process for the removal of antibiotics from water, with low-cost, eco-friendliness, high efficiency and sustainability [7]. In this field, many semiconductors, like TiO2 [8], C3N4 [9] and BiVO4 [10], have been widely investigated. However, the photocatalytic method is still limited in practical application owing to the poor quantum yield caused by the fast recombination between the photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+), together with the difficult regeneration of photocatalysts [11]. The photoelectrochemical (PEC) process, in which the photocatalysts were anchored on a conductive substrate thus avoiding complicated recovery process, was considered as an ideal alternative for wastewater treatment [12]. On the other hand, benefitting from the assistance of applied potential, the photogenerated charges could be efficiently migrated to the counter electrode, and thus achieving the efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs [13,14]. So far, many n-type semiconductor-based photoelectrodes, such as TiO2 [15], WO3 [16], Fe2O3 [17] and BiVO4 [18], have been successfully developed for PEC degradation of antibiotics. Among these photoelectrodes, BiVO4 has attracted considerable attention due to its suitable band gap (∼2.4 eV), preferable band-edge positions, non-toxicity and moderate stability [19]. Nevertheless, more than 60 % of the photogenerated charge carriers in BiVO4 were deactivated owing to the serious charge recombination, slow electron and hole mobilities, as well as sluggish surface reaction kinetics [20].

To boost the PEC performances of BiVO4 photoelectrodes, various strategies, including elemental doping [21], surface modification [22], morphology control [23] and heterojunction construction [24], have been developed. The construction of type II heterojunction was one of the most efficient methods to overcome the limitations of BiVO4, with the promoted spatial separation of photogenerated charges, the improved light absorption and the enlarged interfacial area. For example, the type II heterojunction of Bi2S3/BiVO4 [25], MoO3/BiVO4 [26], Fe2TiO5/BiVO4 [27] and In2O3/BiVO4 [28] have demonstrated significantly boosted PEC activity for water splitting. However, these kind type II BiVO4-based heterojunction photoelectrodes are still rarely reported for the PEC degradation of antibiotics in wastewater. Yu et al. reported a coral-like WO3/BiVO4 with a high removal efficiency of sulfamethoxazole in dual-functional PEC-Cl system [29]. Yang et al. reported BiVO4/WO3 film displayed a higher PEC degradation of norfloxacin [30]. It was thus anticipated that the fabrication of a type II BiVO4-based heterojunction would be of great importance for the removal of antibiotics from wastewater. FeOOH is an ideal iron-based semiconductor for the construction of heterojunctions owing to its superior chemical stability. However, when FeOOH was used as a surface modifier, it still suffers from poor charge transfer with lower PEC performance [31]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which would act as an ultrafast charge channel to accelerate the holes transfer, have been successfully used in the systems of CQDs/TiO2 [32], CQDs/WO3 [33] and CQDs/BiVO4 [34]. However, there are still few reports on the intercalation of CQDs as a hole transfer channel in type II heterojunction photoanode. Therefore, it is significantly expected to deliver the merits of CQDs for accelerating surface hole transfer of the type II BiVO4-based heterojunction photoelectrodes, such as FeOOH/BiVO4. More importantly, the systematic optimization on the PEC behaviors of such photoelectrode for degradation of antibiotics is still lack of deserved attention.

In this work, a type II FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction photoelectrode was fabricated via a facile hydrothermal process, in which the growth of FeOOH over BiVO4 was modulated with CQDs. FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibited excellent PEC performance toward the degradation of a typical antibiotic-TC in aqueous solution, comparing with that of BiVO4. The investigations revealed the formation of type II heterojunction was beneficial to the charge separation, while CQDs could modulate the growth of FeOOH to expose much more active sites and at the same time act as hole-transfer mediator to facilitate the hole transfer from FeOOH/BiVO4 to electrolyte. Furthermore, the reactive species and the intermediates during PEC degradation of TC were identified with radical trapping experiments and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry. This work presented an in-depth understanding of PEC degradation of TC over a heterojunction photoanode, which exhibited great potential to be used as a new path for the removal of TC from aqueous solutions.

The fabrication of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 photoelectrode was illustrated in Fig. 1 a, and the experimental details could be found in Supplementary Materials. Typically, CQDs solution was previously prepared by a direct pyrolyzation of citric acid monohydrate (CA) at 200 °C followed by being dispersed with NaOH solution. Fig. S1 displays CQDs solution with bright blue luminescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm. Fig. S2 indicates CQDs with sphere shape of a size of ca. 3 nm. The morphology and optical property suggested CQDs has been successfully prepared. Meanwhile, BiVO4 photoelectrode was fabricated via an electrodeposition of BiOI and subsequent calcination with VO(acac)2. Fig. 1b shows BiVO4 with worm-like nanorod with the size of ∼ 140 nm. No significant changes on the morphology of BiVO4 were observed after the decoration of only CQDs (Fig. S3). While a large number of FeOOH nanorods with a length of ∼ 80 nm and an average diameter of ∼ 15 nm were grown on the surface of BiVO4 in the presence of Fe2(SO4)3 (Fig. 1c and S4). Interestingly, when FeOOH and CQDs were simultaneously present in the precursor solution, the smaller nanoparticles of FeOOH-CQDs were compactly grown on the surface BiVO4 (Fig. 1d and 1e). The significant change on the morphology of FeOOH species would be mainly attributed to the rich active sites of CQDs for regulating the growth of FeOOH crystal nucleus. This rough surface would be feasible to the mass transfer and act as active sites for TC degradation. The elemental mappings from the surface of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 revealed that FeOOH was dispersed uniformly throughout BiVO4 (Fig. S5). The further TEM image indicated FeOOH-CQDs nanoparticle contacted closely to BiVO4 (Fig. 1f). High resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows the lattice spacings of 0.300 nm and 0.248 nm, matching well with (121) plane of BiVO4 and (111) plane of α-FeOOH (Fig. 1g). Moreover, CQDs were also observed randomly dispersed in FeOOH. Such structure would be beneficial to the photogenerated charge transfer.

The crystalline phase of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 was examined by using X-ray diffractometer (XRD). As seen in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks at 2θ of 26.6°, 37.9°, 51.8° and 65.8° could be indexed to (110), (200), (211) and (301) planes of tetragonal SnO2 from F-doped tin oxide in the substrate (JCPDS No. 46–1088), while the peaks at 2θ of 18.9°, 28.9° and 30.5° matched well with (110), (121) and (040) planes of monoclinic scheelite BiVO4 (JCPDS No. 14–0688). No other characteristic diffraction peaks of FeOOH and CQDs appeared in FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4, which could be ascribed to the low loading or the perfect crystal orientation of the deposited FeOOH. To reveal the crystalline phase of FeOOH species, the residues received from the reaction system for the fabrication of FeOOH/BiVO4 and FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 were further examined with XRD. As shown in Fig. S6a, the diffraction peaks at 2θ of 21.2° and 36.6° clearly matched well with (110) and (111) planes of α-phase FeOOH (JCPDS No.29–0713). Once CQDs was introduced, the diffraction peaks turned into too weaker to be observed (Fig. S6b), which would be assigned to the nanoscale size of FeOOH or the incorporation of CQDs into α-FeOOH. The optical property for FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 was evaluated by using UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS). As shown in Fig. 2b, BiVO4 exhibits optical absorption edge at approximate 510 nm, which is attributed to the intrinsic direct band gap. After being decorated with FeOOH or FeOOH-CQDs, the optical absorption edge was redshifted to 550 and 580 nm for FeOOH/BiVO4 and FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 respectively. By taking the negligible light adsorption of CQDs in visible region into account, it could be concluded the enhanced light absorption of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 photoelectrode was ascribed to the instinct property of FeOOH. Fig. S7 displays the photographs of the fabricated photoelectrodes, further verifying the effects of CQDs and FeOOH on the light absorption of photoelectrodes. By using Kubelka-Munk formula of (αhν)n = A(hν − E g), the bandgap energies (E g) of FeOOH, BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4 and FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 were calculated to be 1.95, 2.48, 2.41 and 2.41 eV respectively [35,36].

The surface chemical states of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 were further examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2d shows a typical XPS spectrum of Bi 4f in BiVO4 with two peaks at 158.9 and 164.2 eV, matching well with Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi (Ⅲ) [37]. With the decoration of FeOOH, Bi 4f exhibits an obvious positive shift of ca. 0.2 eV, suggesting a charge loss at Bi sites in BiVO4. This phenomenon became more profound with ca. 0.5 eV positive shift for FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4, indicating CQDs could significantly enhance the interaction between BiVO4 and FeOOH. Additionally, the binding energies of V 2p at 516.4 and 524.2 eV in BiVO4 also positively shifted to ca. 516.7 and 524.5 eV for FeOOH/BiVO4 and FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4(Fig. S8a), further verifying the strong interaction between BiVO4 and FeOOH [38]. Fig. 2e shows the high resolution XPS spectra for Fe 2p in FeOOH/BiVO4 and FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4, where the peaks around 724.3 and 710.8 eV, accompanying with two satellite bands at 733.3 and 719.6 eV, could be indexed to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2, respectively. The deconvolution of Fe 2p3/2 in FeOOH/BiVO4 would derivate two characteristic peaks at 712.8 and 710.7 eV, which could be assigned to Fe(II) and Fe(III) correspondingly, suggesting the coexistence of Fe(II) and Fe(III) in FeOOH [39]. The presence of Fe(II) might be attributed to the formation of surface oxygen vacancies caused by the reduced coordination of Fe sites in FeOOH. Notably, the binding energies of Fe 2p exhibited a significant negative shift in FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4, suggesting an increased electronic occupation of Fe 2p caused from CQDs regulation. Such change on the electric structure Fe sites would be beneficial for the hole transfer in FeOOH-CQDs overlayer. Fig. 2f displays O 1 s spectra in various samples. As seen, BiVO4 exhibited two peaks at the binding energy of 530.9 and 529.5 eV, corresponding to surface hydroxyl (–OH) and lattice oxygen respectively [40,41]. While O 1 s for both FeOOH/BiVO4 and FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 showed three peaks at 532.3, 531.3 and 529.8 eV, matching well with O in adsorbed H2O, O in hydroxyl group (–OH) and the lattice O (Fe-O, V-O and Bi-O) [42]. C 1 s spectra in Fig. S8b clearly shows a binding energy at 288.6 eV of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4, which could be assigned to C in O = C-O group of CQDs [43]. However, no obvious signals were observed in CQDs/BiVO4 sample, which suggested CQDs was deposited with the growth of FeOOH and CQDs probably existed in the bulk of FeOOH.

The PEC property of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 was firstly examined by using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in 0.1 M Na2SO4 containing 15 mg L−1 TC, as well as that of control samples like BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4. As shown in Fig. S9, in dark all BiVO4-based photoelectrodes exhibited the negligible current at the bias potential range of −0.3 ∼ 1.2 V vs. SCE, while the obvious anode currents generated at 1.2 ∼ 1.4 V vs. SCE. Such currents undoubtedly could be ascribed to the electrochemical water oxidation. Fig. 3 a exhibits FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 possesses the highest photocurrent than that of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 in the potential range of −0.3 ∼ 1.4 V vs. SCE, which strongly indicated the synergistic effect of FeOOH and CQDs on the boosted PEC performance of BiVO4. To further understand the boosted performance of BiVO4, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA), which could be estimated with the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cd1) in non-Faradaic region, was investigated by using cyclic voltammetry (Fig. S10). By plotting the half capacitive current difference (Δj/2=(j a-j b)/2) at 0.25 V vs. SCE versus the scanning rates (Fig. 3b), Cdl for FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 was calculated to be 0.41 mF cm−2, which was significantly higher than that of BiVO4 (0.28 mF cm−2), FeOOH/BiVO4 (0.20 mF cm−2) and CQDs/BiVO4 (0.13 mF cm−2). Such result indicated CQDs could mediate the growth of FeOOH to achieve high exposed active sites, in line with SEM analysis.

The interfacial charge separation kinetics was studied by using the open-circuit potential (OCP). As illustrated in Fig. 3c, OCP of BiVO4 in dark is located at 0.05 V vs. SCE, while that for FeOOH/BiVO4 and FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 is 0.15 and 0.22 V vs. SCE respectively. These positive shifts could be attributed to the physical passivation effect of FeOOH and CQDs, which reduced the intrinsic surface states (SS) on BiVO4 [44,45]. Once the light was switched on, OCP values for all photoelectrodes shifted negatively, in accordance with the character of n-type semiconductor [46]. In addition, the difference of OCP, which expressed as ΔOCP = OCPdark − OCPlight, on various photoelectrodes was also illustrated. As shown in Fig. 3c, FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibited the highest ΔOCP (0.467 V), higher than that of FeOOH/BiVO4 (0.388 V) and BiVO4 (0.349 V). This result clearly indicated the largest band bending at the photoelectrode surface, which would ultimately cause a larger internal electric field at the photoelectrode/electrolyte interface and thus benefit the charge separation and transfer.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was further conducted to clarify the charge transfer kinetics over the surface of these photoelectrodes (Fig. 3d). As shown, a typical one-semicircle, which corresponded to the charge transfer resistance at the photoelectrode/electrolyte interface, Nyquist EIS plot was received for all photoelectrodes. By using the equivalent circuit in Fig. 3d, where R s, R ct and C bulk represent the series resistance of electrolyte and external circuit, the charge-transfer resistance at the interface of semiconductor/electrolyte, and the constant phase element respectively, R ct values for all photoelectrodes were received in the order of BiVO4 (176.6 Ω) > CQDs/BiVO4 (137.0 Ω) > FeOOH/BiVO4 (110.8 Ω) > FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 (69.2 Ω) (Table S1). According to literatures, the lower the charge transfer resistance is, the faster the charge transfer kinetics is [47,48]. It thus revealed that the synergistic effect of CQDs and FeOOH played important roles on the boosted charge transfer at the photoelectrode/electrolyte interface in FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4.

To further explore the nature that enhanced charge separation and transfer of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4, Mott-Schottky (M−S) tests were carried out. As shown in Fig. 3e, both FeOOH and BiVO4 exhibited positive slopes, suggesting FeOOH and BiVO4 are n-type in nature. Correspondingly, the flat band potentials (E fb) for both FeOOH and BiVO4 were plotted and evaluated to be ca. 0.21 and 0.46 V vs. RHE. According to the literatures, E fb for n-type semiconductor is typically 0.1 V higher than the conduction band (CB) [49]. Therefore, CB for FeOOH and BiVO4 were estimated to be approximate 0.11 and 0.36 V vs. RHE respectively. Correspondingly, valence bands (VB) for FeOOH and BiVO4 were received as 2.06 V and 2.84 V vs. RHE. Meanwhile, the carrier densities (N d) of various photoelectrodes were calculated in the order of BiVO4 (1.66 × 1021 cm−3) < FeOOH/BiVO4 (2.43 × 1021 cm−3) < FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 (3.57 × 1021 cm−3), suggesting FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 possessed the optimal charge separation performance.

Based on the above analysis, a type II band alignment between FeOOH and BiVO4 was proposed (Fig. 3f). With the type-II heterojunction, the photogenerated holes in VB of BiVO4 would spontaneously migrate into the VB of FeOOH, while the photogenerated electrons in CB of FeOOH were transferred into the CB of BiVO4, thus significantly boosted charge separation and charge transfer in the photoelectrode. As a result, PEC performance of BiVO4 was significantly enhanced by the formation of type-II heterojunction with FeOOH.

To test the best operation conditions, the PEC degradation of TC was first conducted at different applied potential of 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0 and 1.2 V vs. SCE. As seen in Fig. S11, PEC TC degradation rate was enhanced with the increase of applied potential from 0.4 to 0.6 V vs. SCE, which could be attributed to the increased charge separation caused by external electric field. However, too high applied potential inversely caused detrimental effect on the degradation of TC, owing to the side reaction of oxygen evolution [50]. To simplify, all the following PEC degradation of TC was performed at 0.6 V vs. SCE. Fig. 4 a comparably exhibits PEC degradation of TC over BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4 and FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4. As shown, the PEC degradation ratio of TC over FeOOH-CQDs/ BiVO4 yielded an efficiency of ca. 99 % within 120 min, while those of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4 were 70 %, 60 % and 92 %, respectively. Impressively, within the first 60 min, the TC degradation ratio over FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 (90 %) was about 1.5, 2.0 and 2.5 times to that over BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4. Meanwhile, FeOOH exhibited negligible PEC degradation toward TC (Fig. S12), indicating the significant enhancement in FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 was primarily attributed to the type-II heterojunction effect. The further comparison of this result with other previous works clearly supported that the present PEC method based on FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 photoelectrode is an alternative efficient process for TC degradation (Table S2). Moreover, FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 displayed the highest degradation rate constant of 0.0359 min−1, which was 3.5, 4.6 and 1.7 times that of BiVO4 (0.0103 min−1), CQDs/BiVO4 (0.0078 min−1) and FeOOH/BiVO4 (0.0194 min−1) respectively (Fig. 4b).

In addition, to evaluate the reusable capability of FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 photoelectrode for PEC degradation of TC, a 6-cycle experiment was conducted under the same conditions (Fig. 4c). As shown, the degradation efficiency of TC remained higher than 98 % in the 6th cycle, which strongly suggested FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 photoelectrode possessed excellent stability during PEC process. Furthermore, tap water and lake water were used to examine the matrix effect of the actual medium on PEC degradation of TC over FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4. In the experiments, a suitable amount of TC stock was spiked into tap water or lake water to achieve 15 mg L−1 TC solution in PEC cell. Fig. 4d illustrated the PEC degradation ratio of TC in both tap water and lake water could reach 90 %, implying a high potential of such PEC configuration to be used in practical processes. The reduction of TC degradation could be attributed to the existing forms of TC in tap water and lake water, as well as the competitive surface reaction between TC and inorganic or organic substances with the active species.

To further ascertain the crucial factors the efficient degradation of TC over FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4, the photocatalysis under AM 1.5G illumination without external bias (PC), the electrocatalysis with applied potential without light irradiation (EC) and PEC for TC degradation were comparably performed. As shown in Fig. 5 a, PC process exhibited ca. 25 % degradation ratio of TC, and EC process showed a negligible degradation of TC, while PEC process exhibited the highest degradation ratio of ca. 99 %. Also, the pseudo-first-order degradation rate constants for PC and EC were correspondingly determined to be 0.0024 min−1 and 0.0004 min−1, which were significantly lower than that of PEC (0.0359 min−1) (Fig. 5b). These results clearly indicated a synergetic effect between PC and EC, resulting in high efficiency of PEC process.

To identify the reactive oxygen species in the PEC degradation of TC, the radical trapping experiments were conducted with EDTA-2Na, p-benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) as scavengers to identify holes (h+), superoxide radical (O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (OH). As shown in Fig. 5c and 5d, the PEC degradation of TC over FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 was dramatically reduced in the presence of EDTA-2Na or BQ, indictive of both h+ and O2 − were the major reactive species for the degradation of TC. While the degradation of TC was moderately suppressed with IPA, suggesting OH contributed insignificantly to TC degradation. The weak contribution of OH compared with that of h+ clearly indicated the degradation of TC at anodic region mainly occurred on the surface of the photoelectrode. On the other hand, the obvious contribution of O2 −, which might be attributed to the electroreduction of O2 at counter electrode, indicated the cathodic reaction were also important for the degradation of TC. For in-depth understanding on the role of O2, a control experiment for PEC TC degradation under continuous bubble with Ar was conducted. As shown in Fig. S13, the removal of dissolved oxygen significantly decreased the degradation of TC, indicating the presence of O2 was very helpful to TC degradation.

To gain an insight on the degradation mechanism of TC during PEC process, the intermediates derived from TC were identified by using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Fig. S14). According to the results, 13 intermediates (P1-P13) were rationalized (Table S3). Correspondingly, two major pathways for PEC degradation of TC were proposed, as schemed in Fig. 6 . For pathway I, TC was first transferred into P1 (m/z = 461) and P2 (m/z = 479) via continuous hydroxylation reaction with OH, and followed by converted into P4 (m/z = 304) via the demethylation, deamidation and ring-opening reactions. For pathway II, P5 (m/z = 416) was first produced via the demethylation in dimethylamino group, and then was transferred into P6 (m/z = 400) via the ring-opening reaction with the loss of –OH. P4 and P6 would further oxidized by h+, O2 − and OH to produce P7 (m/z = 189), P8 (m/z = 246), P9 (m/z = 202) and P10 (m/z = 160). With the continuous reaction, the above intermediates would be further oxidized with ring-opening to generate P11 (m/z = 126), P12 (m/z = 144) and P13 (m/z = 102), which would be further mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

In summary, a highly homogeneous FeOOH-CQDs overlayer was in-situ anchored on the surface of BiVO4 via a facile hydrothermal route. The investigations indicated a type-II heterojunction was formed between FeOOH-CQDs overlayer and BiVO4 substrate. Benefiting from the type-II heterojunction, the charge separation over BiVO4 photoelectrode was improved; meanwhile with the assistance of CQDs, the growth of FeOOH, as well as the charge transfer between FeOOH and BiVO4, were mediated. The synergistic contribution of FeOOH and CQDs made FeOOH-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibit efficient PEC degradation toward TC, with a notable degradation efficiency of ca. 99 % within 120 min. The findings in this work will open a new pathway for the removal of TC from aqueous solutions.

Shuai Zhu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xiaohui Li: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Qinge Fan: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Penglong Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yanfeng Pu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yushuai Jia: Visualization, Formal analysis. Li Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Zhixian Chang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ruifeng Chong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
